**Menorca**[[a]](#cite_note-1) or **Minorca**[[b]](#cite_note-2) (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Insula Minor*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'smaller island', later *Minorica*) is one of the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands") located in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") belonging to Spain. Its name derives from its size, contrasting it with nearby [Mallorca](/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") (Majorca). Its capital is [Maó](/wiki/Ma%C3%B3 "Maó"), situated on the island's eastern end, although Menorca is not a province and forms a political union with the other islands in the archipelago. [Ciutadella de Menorca](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca") and [Maó](/wiki/Mah%C3%B3n "Mahón") are the main ports and largest towns.

Per the Census of 1 January 2025, Menorca had a population of 102,477.[[1]](#cite_note-3) Its highest point, a hill in the middle of the island named [El Toro](/wiki/El_Toro_(Minorca) "El Toro (Minorca)") (from Catalan "*turó*" meaning *hill*), is 358 metres (1,175 feet) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The island is known for its collection of [megalithic](/wiki/European_megalithic_culture "European megalithic culture") stone monuments: [*navetes*](/wiki/Naveta "Naveta"), [*taules*](/wiki/Taula "Taula") and *[talaiots](/wiki/Talaiot "Talaiot")*, which indicate very early [prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistoric "Prehistoric") human activity. Some of the earliest culture on Menorca was influenced by other [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") cultures, including the Greek [Minoans](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization") of [ancient Crete](/wiki/Ancient_Crete "Ancient Crete") (see also [Gymnesian Islands](/wiki/Gymnesian_Islands "Gymnesian Islands")). For example, the use of inverted plastered timber columns at [Knossos](/wiki/Knossos "Knossos") is thought to have influenced early peoples of Menorca in imitating this practice.[[2]](#cite_note-4)

The end of the [Punic Wars](/wiki/Punic_Wars "Punic Wars") saw an increase in piracy in the western Mediterranean. The [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") occupation of [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania") had meant a growth of maritime trade between the [Iberian](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") and [Italian peninsulas](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula"). Pirates took advantage of the strategic location of the Balearic Islands to raid Roman commerce, using both Menorca and [Mallorca](/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") as bases. In reaction to this, the Romans [invaded Menorca](/wiki/Invasion_of_Minorca "Invasion of Minorca"). By 123 BC, both islands were fully under Roman control, later being incorporated into the province of [Hispania Citerior](/wiki/Hispania_Citerior "Hispania Citerior").

In 13 BC, Roman emperor [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") reorganised the provincial system and the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands") became part of the [Tarraconensis](/wiki/Tarraconensis "Tarraconensis") imperial province. The ancient town of Mago ([Mahón](/wiki/Mah%C3%B3n "Mahón")) was transformed from a [Carthaginian](/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage") to a Roman town.[[3]](#cite_note-5)

### Jews of Menorca

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Jews of Menorca")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Minorca_by_Piri_Reis.jpg/250px-Minorca_by_Piri_Reis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minorca_by_Piri_Reis.jpg)

Historic map of Minorca by [Piri Reis](/wiki/Piri_Reis "Piri Reis")

The island had a Jewish population.[[4]](#cite_note-Elukin,_Jonathan_M_2007-6) The *Letter on the Conversion of the Jews* by a fifth-century bishop named [Severus](/wiki/Severus_of_Minorca "Severus of Minorca") tells of the [forced conversion](/wiki/Converso "Converso") of the island's 540 Jewish men and women in AD 418.[[5]](#cite_note-7) Several Jews, including Theodore, a rich representative Jew who stood high in the estimation of his coreligionists and of Christians alike, underwent baptism. The act of conversion brought about, within a previously peaceful coexisting community, the expulsion of the ruling Jewish elite into the bleak hinterlands, the burning of synagogues, and the gradual reinstatement of certain Jewish families after the forced acceptance of Christianity, allowing the survival of those Jewish families who had not already perished.[[4]](#cite_note-Elukin,_Jonathan_M_2007-6) Many Jews secretly retained their Jewish faith while outwardly professing Christian beliefs. Some of these Jews form part of the [Xueta](/wiki/Xueta "Xueta") community.

When Menorca became a British possession in 1713, they actively encouraged the immigration of foreign non-Catholics, which included Jews who were not accepted by the predominantly Christian inhabitants. When the Jewish community in Mahon requested the use of a room as a synagogue, their request was refused, and they were denounced by the clergy. In 1781, when [Louis des Balbes de Berton de Crillon, duc de Mahon](/wiki/Louis_des_Balbes_de_Berton_de_Crillon,_duc_de_Mahon "Louis des Balbes de Berton de Crillon, duc de Mahon") invaded Menorca, he ordered all Jews to leave in four days. At that time, the local, around 500-strong, Jewish community was transported from Menorca in four Spanish ships to the port of [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille").[[6]](#cite_note-8)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

The [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") easily conquered the island in the fifth century. The [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") recovered it in 534. Following the [Umayyad conquest of Hispania](/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_Hispania "Umayyad conquest of Hispania"), Menorca was annexed to the [Caliphate of Córdoba](/wiki/Caliphate_of_Cordoba "Caliphate of Cordoba") in 903, with many [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") emigrating to the island.

*Manûrqa* ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): منورقة) was the Arabicized name given to the island by the [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") from its annexation to the [Caliphate of Cordoba](/wiki/Caliphate_of_Cordoba "Caliphate of Cordoba") by 'Isâm al-Khawlânî in 903 until the rule of the last Muslim ra'îs, [Abû 'Umar ibn Sa'îd](/wiki/Ab%C3%BB_%27Umar_ibn_Sa%27%C3%AEd "Abû 'Umar ibn Sa'îd") in 1287. The only urban centre of the island was *Madînat al Jazîra* or *al Manûrqa* (modern [Ciutadella](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca")). Most of the population lived in small farm communities organized under a [tribal](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") structure.

In 1231, after Christian forces took Mallorca, Menorca chose to become an independent Islamic state, albeit one tributary to King [James I of Aragon](/wiki/James_I_of_Aragon "James I of Aragon"). The island was ruled first by [Abû 'Uthmân Sa'îd Hakam al Qurashi](/wiki/Ab%C3%BB_%27Uthm%C3%A2n_Sa%27%C3%AEd_Hakam_al_Qurashi "Abû 'Uthmân Sa'îd Hakam al Qurashi") (1234–1282), and following his death by his son, [Abû 'Umar ibn Sa'îd](/wiki/Ab%C3%BB_%27Umar_ibn_Sa%27%C3%AEd "Abû 'Umar ibn Sa'îd") (1282–1287).

A [Catalan-Aragonese](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon") invasion, led by [Alfonso III](/wiki/Alfonso_III_of_Aragon "Alfonso III of Aragon") (also known as [Count of Barcelona](/wiki/Count_of_Barcelona "Count of Barcelona") Alfons II), came on 17 January 1287; its anniversary is now celebrated as Menorca's national day. Once the island was captured, most of its Muslim inhabitants were enslaved and sold in the [slave](/wiki/Slavery#Medieval_Europe "Slavery") markets of Eivissa, [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_(city_in_Spain) "Valencia (city in Spain)") and [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), while others became Christians.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

After the Christian conquest of 1287, the island was part of the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon"). For some time it was ceded to the [Kingdom of Mallorca](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mallorca "Kingdom of Mallorca"), a vassal state of the Crown, but it was retaken by the king of Aragon in 1343. Eventually the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon") merged with the [Crown of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile"), and so Menorca became part of Spain.

During the 16th century, [Turkish](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") naval attacks [destroyed Mahon](/wiki/Sack_of_Mah%C3%B3n "Sack of Mahón"), and the then capital, [Ciutadella](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca"). In Mahon, [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates") from North Africa took considerable booty and as many as 6,000 slaves.[[8]](#cite_note-10) Various Spanish kings, including [Philip III](/wiki/Philip_III_of_Spain "Philip III of Spain") and [Philip IV](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_Spain "Philip IV of Spain"), styled themselves "King of Minorca" as a subsidiary title.

Anglo-Dutch forces [captured Menorca](/wiki/Capture_of_Minorca_(1708) "Capture of Minorca (1708)") in 1708 during the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"). The island became a British possession and was formally ceded to Great Britain by Spain in the 1713 [Peace of Utrecht](/wiki/Peace_of_Utrecht "Peace of Utrecht"). Governed by a succession of [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") officers, during the period of British rule Menorca's capital was moved to [Mahón](/wiki/Mah%C3%B3n "Mahón") and a [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") base established in the town's harbour. Menorca remained a British possession until 1756, when French forces [captured it](/wiki/Siege_of_Fort_St_Philip_(1756) "Siege of Fort St Philip (1756)") during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") after [repulsing a British relief attempt](/wiki/Battle_of_Minorca_(1756) "Battle of Minorca (1756)"). However, [Britain's victory in the war](/wiki/Great_Britain_in_the_Seven_Years%27_War "Great Britain in the Seven Years' War") led the island to be ceded back to them in the 1763 [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"). In 1782, Franco-Spanish forces [captured the island](/wiki/Invasion_of_Minorca_(1781) "Invasion of Minorca (1781)") during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), and Britain ceded the island back to Spain in the 1783 [Peace of Paris](/wiki/Peace_of_Paris_(1783) "Peace of Paris (1783)"). The British once again [captured Menorca](/wiki/Capture_of_Minorca_(1798) "Capture of Minorca (1798)") in 1798 during the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), though they ceded it back to Spain in 1802 [Treaty of Amiens](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amiens "Treaty of Amiens").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Renewed Spanish rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Renewed Spanish rule")]

As with the rest of the Balearic Islands, Menorca was not occupied by the French during the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"), as it was successfully protected by the Royal Navy, this time allied to Spain.

A quarantine station ([lazaretto](/wiki/Lazaretto "Lazaretto")), Llatzaret (Catalan), was constructed from 1793 to 1807 next to the entrance to the Port Mahon. It served ships from North Africa wishing to reach the Iberian Peninsula or the ports of the Balearic Islands. Lazarettos confined the crews of ships that were suspected of carrying infectious diseases, such as the plague. The crew needed to spend up to 40 days within its walls until it was clear there was no infection or until the sick recovered. It is now a national monument and can only be reached as part of an official tour.[[9]](#cite_note-11)[*[circular reference](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia_and_sources_that_mirror_or_use_it "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

From 1815 until the mid-19th century, the U.S. Navy developed its Mediterranean headquarters at Port Mahon,[[10]](#cite_note-12) leaving behind the [English Cemetery](/wiki/English_Cemetery,_Menorca "English Cemetery, Menorca"), which was restored by the [Spanish government](/wiki/Spanish_government "Spanish government") in 2008 and is maintained in the 21st century.

### Since 1900

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Since 1900")]

During the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), Menorca stayed loyal to the [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic"), while the rest of the Balearic Islands supported the Spanish [Nationalists](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)"). The island did not see ground combat, but it was a target of aerial bombing by the pro-Nationalist Italian [Corpo Truppe Volontarie](/wiki/Corpo_Truppe_Volontarie "Corpo Truppe Volontarie"). Many Menorcans were also killed when taking part in a failed [invasion of Mallorca](/wiki/Battle_of_Mallorca "Battle of Mallorca"). During the Pedro Marqués Barber era (July–December 1936) some Mallorcans and a priest were executed on the island. After the Nationalist victory in the [Battle of Minorca](/wiki/Battle_of_Minorca_(1939) "Battle of Minorca (1939)") in February 1939, the Royal Navy assisted in a [peaceful transition of power](/wiki/Peaceful_transition_of_power "Peaceful transition of power") in Menorca and the evacuation of some political refugees aboard [HMS *Devonshire*](/wiki/HMS_Devonshire_(39) "HMS Devonshire (39)").

In October 1993, Menorca was designated by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a [biosphere reserve](/wiki/Biosphere_reserve "Biosphere reserve"). In July 2005, the island's application to become the 25th member of the [International Island Games Association](/wiki/International_Island_Games_Association "International Island Games Association") was approved.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

As the major part of Balearic Islands, Menorca has a [mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *[Csa](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate")*), with mild winters and hot summers. Menorca is generally wetter than Mallorca, with rainfall peaking in late autumn. Average annual highs range between 14 °C (57 °F) in winter to 29 °C (84 °F) in summer. Due to its offshore position and the small size of the island, temperatures are generally quite stable.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Mahon-Hafen.jpg/250px-Mahon-Hafen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahon-Hafen.jpg)

Port Mahon (Mahón)

The location of Menorca in the middle of the western Mediterranean was a staging point for the different cultures since prehistoric times. This Balearic Island has a mix of colonial and local architecture.

The *festes* take place throughout the summer in different towns around the island, and have their origins in the early 14th century.[[15]](#cite_note-17) The international opera week and international organ festival in Mahón, and the summer music festival and Capella Davidica concerts in Ciutadella are the main events of the island.

Menorca's cuisine is dominated by the [Mediterranean diet](/wiki/Mediterranean_diet "Mediterranean diet"), which is known to be very healthy.[[16]](#cite_note-18) While many of the locals have adopted modern attitudes they still uphold certain old traditions.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

### Traditional celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Traditional celebrations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Menorca_jaleo.jpg/250px-Menorca_jaleo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Menorca_jaleo.jpg)

[Menorquín horse](/wiki/Menorqu%C3%ADn_horse "Menorquín horse") ridden by caixer at festes

Menorca is especially well known for its traditional summer "festes", which intrigue many visitors. The [Saint John's Feast](/wiki/Saint_John%27s_Eve "Saint John's Eve") is held annually in [Ciutadella de Menorca](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca"), during 23–25 June. The festes last for three days. On the first day, a man bears a well-groomed sheep upon his shoulders and parades around the local streets. In the late evening, main streets are closed, and bonfires held upon them.

On the second day, [locally bred black horses](/wiki/Menorqu%C3%ADn_horse "Menorquín horse") are dressed with ribbons and rosettes. The riders, or "caixers", ride the horses through the streets and, along with a tumultuous crowd of people, encourage them to rear up on their hind legs. The brave can be found running underneath them in an attempt to touch the horses hearts for good luck. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The third day sees intense competition between the riders in a harmless form of [jousting](/wiki/Jousting "Jousting") that involves spearing a suspended ring with a lance at considerable speed. The festes are brought to a close with a firework display.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sports")]

As a small island, Menorca offers limited opportunities to see top-level sport competitions. Football in Menorca is played at the fifth level of the Spanish football pyramid. There are currently 11 clubs contesting the [Regional Preferente de Menorca](/wiki/Divisiones_Regionales_de_F%C3%BAtbol_in_Balearic_Islands "Divisiones Regionales de Fútbol in Balearic Islands"), the champion of which progresses to the [Tercera División](/wiki/Tercera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Tercera División") Grupo XI playoffs. The winner of this playoff is promoted to Tercera División; the last Menorquí club to do so was [CF Sporting Mahonés](/wiki/CF_Sporting_Mahon%C3%A9s "CF Sporting Mahonés") in 2009.

[CV Ciutadella](/wiki/CV_Ciutadella "CV Ciutadella") are a professional women's volleyball club who play in the [Superliga Femenina](/wiki/Superliga_Femenina_de_Voleibol "Superliga Femenina de Voleibol"), the top league of Spanish volleyball, having won the league championship in 2011 and 2012. They play at Pavelló Municipal d'Esports in [Ciutadella](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca").

A semi-professional basketball club, [CB Menorca](/wiki/CB_Menorca "CB Menorca"), play in the [LEB Plata](/wiki/LEB_Plata "LEB Plata"), the third level of Spanish basketball. Their home court is [Pavelló Menorca](/wiki/Pavell%C3%B3_Menorca "Pavelló Menorca") in the Bintaufa neighborhood just outside of Maó.

In recent years, some sporting events that gather hundreds of participants have been successfully held on a yearly basis, such as the triathlon race [Extreme Man Menorca](/w/index.php?title=Extreme_Man_Menorca&action=edit&redlink=1 "Extreme Man Menorca (page does not exist)") and the single-staged ultramarathon race [Trail Menorca Camí de Cavalls](/wiki/Trail_Menorca_Cam%C3%AD_de_Cavalls "Trail Menorca Camí de Cavalls"). In 2014, it was announced that the island would host the 18th editions of the [Island Games](/wiki/Island_Games "Island Games") in 2019; however, Menorca later pulled out of hosting the event, citing a change of government as the main reason.[[18]](#cite_note-20)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Language")]

The two official languages are [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") and Spanish.[[19]](#cite_note-21) Natives to the island speak the variety of Catalan called *Menorquí*, and Spanish as well; many residents originating from the mainland are monolingual in Spanish. The language of education and of government is [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), with Spanish taught alongside it.

A 2014 survey carried out by the government of the Balearic Islands found that 53.5% of participants identified themselves as Catalan speakers, 36.7% as Spanish speakers, and 7.7% as bilingual speakers.[[20]](#cite_note-22)

The most distinctive difference between *Menorquí* and standard Catalan, as with most [Balearic dialects](/wiki/Balearic_dialect "Balearic dialect"), is the word used for the article "the", where *Menorquí* uses "es" for masculine and "sa" for feminine. Menorquí thus shares the source of its article with many Sardinian varieties (masc. sing. *su*, fem sing. *sa*), rather than the standard Catalan "el" and "la", similar to other Romance languages (e.g. Spanish *el*, *la*, Italian *il*, *la*), corresponding to a form which was historically used along the [Costa Brava](/wiki/Costa_Brava "Costa Brava") of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), from where it is supposed that the islands were repopulated after being conquered from the [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors").

Menorquí also has a few English [loan words](/wiki/Loan_word "Loan word") dating back to the period of British rule, such as "grevi", "xumaquer", "boinder" and "xoc" taken from "gravy", "shoemaker", "bow window" and "chalk", respectively.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

### Food and drink

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Food and drink")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Gin_Xoriguer.jpg/250px-Gin_Xoriguer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gin_Xoriguer.jpg)

Bottle of Gin Xoriguer, the typical gin from Menorca. It is very often mixed with lemonade.

Wine production has been known on the island since ancient times, but it went into a heavy decline over the last century. Now, several new, small wineries have started up, producing wines locally.[[22]](#cite_note-Vinologue-24)

Lingering British influence is seen in the Menorcans' taste for [gin](/wiki/Gin "Gin"), which during local *festes* honoring towns' [patron saints](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") is mixed with lemonade (or [bitter lemon](/wiki/Bitter_lemon "Bitter lemon")) to make a golden liquid known as *Pomada*. Gin from Menorca (known as Gin de Minorca or Gin de Mahón)
is not derived from grain alcohol but from wine alcohol ([eau de vie de vin](/wiki/Eau_de_vie "Eau de vie")), making it more akin to brandy. It has the distinction to have [geographical identity protection](/wiki/Geographical_indication "Geographical indication"). Probably the best known gin is Gin Xoriguer which is named after the typical Menorcan windmill which was used to make the first gin.

[Mayonnaise](/wiki/Mayonnaise "Mayonnaise") is thought to take its name from the capital of the island. According to this theory, it was first prepared by a French chef in 1756 as part of a victory feast after the capture of [Port Mahon](/wiki/Mah%C3%B3n "Mahón"), Minorca. In those days, sauces were prepared by combining cream and eggs. The French chef needed cream to prepare a sauce, but there was none to be found. He therefore replaced it with olive oil, creating mayonnaise. The word ‘mayonnaise’ is the French for ‘mahonesa’, meaning from or belonging to [Mahón](/wiki/Mah%C3%B3n "Mahón") (‘Mahon’ in French).[[23]](#cite_note-Food_Chronology-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26)

Also famous is [Mahón cheese](/wiki/Mah%C3%B3n_cheese "Mahón cheese"), "formatge de Maó", typical of the island.

Sweets known as [flaons](/wiki/Flaons "Flaons") are one of the typical gastronomic products of Menorca.

## Wildlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Wildlife")]

### Flowers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Flowers")]

Menorca is rich in wild flowers with over 900 species of flowering plants recorded. Many are those typical of the Mediterranean, but some are endemic. There are 24 or 25 species of orchid found and of these most flower early in the year in late March, April and May.

### Insects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Insects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Cleopatra_butterfly_menorca.jpg/250px-Cleopatra_butterfly_menorca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cleopatra_butterfly_menorca.jpg)

Cleopatra, Algendar gorge

30 species of butterflies have been recorded on Menorca and most are on the wing from March to late September. The species that occur include the [Cleopatra](/wiki/Gonepteryx_cleopatra "Gonepteryx cleopatra"), [Lang's short tailed blue](/wiki/Leptotes_pirithous "Leptotes pirithous") and the [two-tailed pasha](/wiki/Charaxes_jasius "Charaxes jasius").
  
Despite not having many large wetlands dragonflies abound on Menorca. Seventeen species have been recorded including the [emperor dragonfly](/wiki/Emperor_dragonfly "Emperor dragonfly").

* [List of butterflies of Menorca](/wiki/List_of_butterflies_of_Menorca "List of butterflies of Menorca")
* [List of dragonflies of Menorca](/wiki/List_of_dragonflies_of_Menorca "List of dragonflies of Menorca")

### Reptiles and amphibians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Reptiles and amphibians")]

There are three species of amphibians: [green toad](/wiki/Bufo_viridis "Bufo viridis") (*Bufo viridis*), [marsh frog](/wiki/Marsh_frog "Marsh frog") and [stripeless tree frog](/wiki/Stripeless_tree_frog "Stripeless tree frog") (*Hyla meridionalis*).
The common lizard seen all over the island is the [Italian wall lizard](/wiki/Italian_wall_lizard "Italian wall lizard") (*Podarcis siculus*) although the [Moroccan rock lizard](/wiki/Moroccan_rock_lizard "Moroccan rock lizard") (*Scelaris perspicillata*) also occurs. The Balearic endemic [Lilford's wall lizard](/wiki/Lilford%27s_wall_lizard "Lilford's wall lizard") (*Podarcis lilfordi*) can be found on many of the offshore islands. Two species of [gecko](/wiki/Gecko "Gecko") can be found on Menorca, the [Moorish](/wiki/Moorish_gecko "Moorish gecko") (*Tarentola mauritanica*) and the [Turkish](/wiki/Mediterranean_house_gecko "Mediterranean house gecko") (*Hemidactylus turcicus*) also called the Mediterranean house gecko.
Four species of snake occur: the [viperine snake](/wiki/Viperine_snake "Viperine snake") (*Natrix maura*), [grass snake](/wiki/Grass_snake "Grass snake"), [false smooth snake](/wiki/False_smooth_snake "False smooth snake") (*Macroprotodon cucullatus*) and the [ladder snake](/wiki/Ladder_snake "Ladder snake") (*Rhinechis scalaris*).

[Hermann's tortoise](/wiki/Hermann%27s_tortoise "Hermann's tortoise") (*Testudo hermanni*) is quite common and can be found all over the island. Two terrapin species are also found, the native [European pond terrapin](/wiki/European_pond_terrapin "European pond terrapin") (*Emys orbicularis*) and the introduced American [red-eared slider](/wiki/Red-eared_slider "Red-eared slider") *(Trachemys scripta*).

### Birds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Birds")]

The birdlife of Menorca is very well known. Menorca is a well watched island which is on the migration route of many species and good number of passage migrants can be seen in spring.[[25]](#cite_note-27) Residents include [Audouin's gull](/wiki/Audouin%27s_gull "Audouin's gull"), [blue rock thrush](/wiki/Blue_rock_thrush "Blue rock thrush") and [Thekla lark](/wiki/Thekla_lark "Thekla lark"). [Booted eagle](/wiki/Booted_eagle "Booted eagle") and [red kite](/wiki/Red_kite "Red kite") are easy to see as is [Egyptian vulture](/wiki/Egyptian_vulture "Egyptian vulture") in the right habitat. In summer there are [bee-eaters](/wiki/Bee-eaters "Bee-eaters") and Menorca has major colonies of [Cory's shearwater](/wiki/Cory%27s_shearwater "Cory's shearwater") and [Balearic shearwater](/wiki/Balearic_shearwater "Balearic shearwater").

### Mammals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Mammals")]

Menorca has no large native mammals. There are some small mammals including rabbits, bats, rats, mice, [pine martens](/wiki/Pine_marten "Pine marten") and a subspecies of [North African hedgehog](/wiki/North_African_hedgehog "North African hedgehog").

## Municipalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Municipalities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Minorca_Municipalities.svg/500px-Minorca_Municipalities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Minorca_Municipalities.svg)

Municipal boundaries in Menorca

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Menorca2021OSM.png/250px-Menorca2021OSM.png)](/wiki/File:Menorca2021OSM.png)

Enlargeable, detailed map of Menorca

The major towns are [Port Mahon](/wiki/Port_Mahon "Port Mahon") and [Ciutadella de Menorca](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca"). The island is administratively divided into eight municipalities (from west to east):

* [Ciutadella de Menorca](/wiki/Ciutadella_de_Menorca "Ciutadella de Menorca") (or just *Ciutadella* locally) – the ancient capital of Menorca until 1722.
* [Ferreries](/wiki/Ferreries "Ferreries")
* [Es Mercadal](/wiki/Es_Mercadal "Es Mercadal")
  + [Fornells](/wiki/Fornells,_Minorca "Fornells, Minorca"), which belongs to the municipality of Es Mercadal. Famous for its [lobster stew](/wiki/Lobster_stew "Lobster stew") (*caldereta*).
* [Es Migjorn Gran](/wiki/Es_Migjorn_Gran "Es Migjorn Gran") (or *Es Mitjorn Gran*) – hometown of [supercentarian](/wiki/Supercentarian "Supercentarian") [Joan Riudavets](/wiki/Joan_Riudavets "Joan Riudavets").
* [Alaior](/wiki/Alaior "Alaior")
  + [Cala En Porter](/wiki/Cala_En_Porter "Cala En Porter") – a tourist and residential area
* [Port Mahon](/wiki/Port_Mahon "Port Mahon") (officially *Maó* in Catalan, *Mahón* in Spanish) – became the capital in 1722 during British rule due to its strategic natural harbour.
  + Llucmassanes – a small [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)") which belongs to the municipality of Maó.
  + Sant Climent, which belongs to the municipality of Maó.
* [Es Castell](/wiki/Es_Castell "Es Castell") – Founded by the British and originally named as Georgetown.
* [Sant Lluís](/wiki/Sant_Llu%C3%ADs "Sant Lluís") – Founded by the French and originally named Saint-Louis.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Menorca&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transport")]

Menorca has several roads that go around the island, the most important one being [Me-1 road (Spain)](/wiki/Me-1_road_(Spain) "Me-1 road (Spain)"), which goes from the island's capital, Mahón, to Ciutadella. Menorca also has many bus lines, most of them only operated in the summer due to tourism. The most important line is line 01, which goes through Me-1 passing through all municipalities except Es Castell and Sant Lluís.

Menorca has one  [Airport](/wiki/Menorca_Airport "Menorca Airport"), that serves to the whole [Island](/wiki/Island "Island").
